headline: Default Codec Allows Arbitrary Class Deserialization (Kryo5Codec)
message: Kryo5Codec is the DEFAULT codec for all Redisson clients. The default constructor passes an empty allowlist, which causes setRegistrationRequired(false). Kryo's readClassAndObject() then reads a fully-qualified class name from the byte stream and instantiates it. An attacker who can write a single value to any Redis key the application reads achieves arbitrary class instantiation and RCE via standard gadget chains (ysoserial Kryo payloads). Every Redisson installation that does not explicitly override the codec is affected.

headline: Distributed Executor Loads Arbitrary Bytecode From Redis
message: The RScheduledExecutorService/RExecutorService feature ships task bytecode through Redis. A worker node calls defineClass() on whatever bytes sit in params.getClassBody(). There is no signature verification, no class allowlist, no sandbox. This is the most direct RCE possible — no gadget chain required. An attacker who can write to the Redis hash backing the executor queue simply submits a TaskParameters containing their own .class bytes. The lambda path (line 321) compounds this: it uses the 2-argument CustomObjectInputStream constructor which never sets allowedClasses.

headline: SerializationCodec Uses Unfiltered ObjectInputStream
message: SerializationCodec's default constructors leave the deserializer wide open. new SerializationCodec() results in a raw ObjectInputStream with zero filtering. new SerializationCodec(classLoader) uses CustomObjectInputStream with allowedClasses == null, so the allowlist check is bypassed. Result: textbook ysoserial RCE on any value read from Redis. Opt-in codec but very commonly chosen by users migrating from other Redis clients.

headline: Jackson Codec: Polymorphic Typing Without Effective Validator
message: JsonJacksonCodec uses the legacy Jackson 2.x default-typing pattern (JsonTypeInfo.Id.CLASS) that reads @class from the JSON payload and instantiates it. No PolymorphicTypeValidator is supplied (null passed). This is the pattern that gave rise to dozens of CVEs (CVE-2017-7525 et seq.). An attacker can write a malicious JSON object to Redis with a @class field pointing to a gadget class (e.g., ClassPathXmlApplicationContext) to achieve RCE on read.

headline: Jackson 3 Codec: Validator Allows All Object Subtypes (No-Op)
message: The Jackson 3 variant appears to use a PolymorphicTypeValidator, but allowIfSubType(Object.class) is a tautology — every Java class satisfies this. The validator provides zero protection. Same exploitation path as the Jackson 2 finding: @class field in JSON controls instantiation, enabling RCE via known gadget classes.

headline: ForyCodec Defaults to Unrestricted Class Deserialization
message: Identical pattern to Kryo5Codec. Apache Fory with class registration disabled reads class names from the byte stream and instantiates them. Default constructor leads to empty allowlist which leads to requireClassRegistration(false). The fory.deserialize() call reads class names from Redis bytes with no restriction.

headline: Legacy KryoCodec Never Requires Class Registration
message: Unlike Kryo5Codec, the legacy KryoCodec doesn't even attempt to enable registration — there is no code path that sets setRegistrationRequired(). Every instance is unrestricted. Same RCE risk as the default codec finding. An attacker who can write to Redis can achieve arbitrary class instantiation.

headline: SnakeYAML Tag Inspector Disabled in Config Loader
message: SnakeYAML 2.x added TagInspector specifically to block YAML tags like !!javax.script.ScriptEngineManager that lead to RCE. This code explicitly disables that protection with tag -> true. Combined with Constructor (not SafeConstructor) and Class.forName(name, true, classLoader) which runs static initializers, a malicious config file achieves RCE. Config.fromYAML(URL) fetches config over the network (SSRF chains), Spring Boot's spring.redis.redisson.config accepts inline YAML (property injection chains), and Tomcat's RedissonSessionManager loads from a configPath attribute (file write chains).

headline: Allowlist Bypass via Proxy Class Resolution
message: Even when a user configures allowedClasses on SerializationCodec, the protection only covers resolveClass(). Java serialization also routes through resolveProxyClass() for dynamic proxies, which has no allowlist check. Proxy-based gadget chains (e.g., AnnotationInvocationHandler patterns) can load arbitrary interface classes, bypassing the allowlist entirely.

headline: Tomcat Session Manager Inherits Codec Deserialization Risk
message: The Tomcat session manager stores session attributes in Redis via RMap<String, Object>. getAttribute() calls map.get() which deserializes via the configured codec. The attack surface is unauthenticated HTTP: an attacker writes a poisoned session attribute to Redis under a known/guessable session key, then makes an HTTP request with that JSESSIONID. Tomcat calls session.getAttribute() which triggers codec deserialization and RCE. The same applies to Principal deserialization. Five Tomcat versions duplicate this code.

headline: JNDI Lookup With Configurable Name
message: The JNDI name comes from Hibernate properties (typically hibernate.cfg.xml / persistence.xml). If an attacker can influence those properties, they could supply ldap://attacker.example/Evil for a Log4Shell-style RCE. Config files are normally as trusted as the application itself, so this is primarily a defense-in-depth concern. The JNDI name is not validated to prevent URL schemes like ldap:, rmi:, dns:, or iiop:.

headline: Optional TLS Verification Bypass
message: When SslVerificationMode.NONE is set and no truststore is configured, InsecureTrustManagerFactory.INSTANCE is used, disabling all certificate verification. Default is SslVerificationMode.STRICT so this is purely opt-in. However, the deprecated boolean setter setSslEnableEndpointIdentification(false) silently downgrades to NONE, which may surprise users who think they're only disabling hostname checks.

headline: SHA-1 Used For Non-Security Purposes
message: SHA-1 is used in three locations: ServiceManager.java:579 for Lua script SHA for Redis EVALSHA (mandated by Redis protocol), RedissonPriorityQueue.java:133 for comparator identity hash, and RedissonSortedSet.java:141 for comparator identity hash. All three uses are non-security — cache keys or protocol requirements, not integrity checks. Not a vulnerability.

headline: Log Injection via Session ID
message: The session ID comes from an HTTP cookie (attacker-controlled) and is interpolated directly into log messages using string concatenation. CRLF in the cookie value could forge log lines. Redisson does not depend on Log4j (uses SLF4J), so this is not a Log4Shell vector — purely a log-forging concern. Should use parameterized logging instead.

headline: TypedJsonJacksonCodec Is The Safe Pattern
message: TypedJsonJacksonCodec (and TypedJsonJackson3Codec) override initTypeInclusion() to be a no-op and require explicit type information at construction. This is the secure pattern for Jackson-based codecs. These should be recommended as the default in documentation over JsonJacksonCodec. Informational positive finding.
